journal: I completed everything on my task lists but that's not the point. No backbone bugs, no adhoc events shows up. I count that a win. Son finished his food, I used the timer method when he wants to pull me away from work, well today's work is pretty chill so I can leave any time, but I still should use the method to set the rules. He seems to accept mommy's working and him playing, mommy will return when the timer beeps. Without debugging the app, I finally have the mental space to think ahead. What's my 6 months marketing plan, how do I make it as seemingly as possible? How are my days evolved around making it happen? Yes, that's what I was thinking while walking with my son and mom.

journal:
Tried the timer method with son, he still need some time getting used to, but after a few tries, he's getting better. At first he didn't like the idea and wanted me to play with him on the floor. I stayed on my work chair, though allowing him to still play with me, and I can hug him whenever he wants, but sitting on my work chair is a must. After a while he left and played on his own. When the timer beeps I showed great excitement to play with him again on the floor.  For me, I looked a few times on the timer when it will end as it itches me there's some work left undone. 

This morning was little devastating, as I saw another backbone bug again, the evening and morning insights somehow didn't show. Although the past few weeks, this sorts of ai insight issues keeps coming back every two days, but I should be glad they appeared now than appear when there's real users. At least I have more experience on how to fix (well just Cursor and deepseek), but I am not familiarize with them and know how to spot them, and if it's finally fixed, then it's good for the long time. I decided to keep the non-urgent bugs to March 4th though, that's when cursor's next month starts, I will only use cursor if it's backbone related bugs. 

Email segmentation has an outstanding progress. I had deepseek help me analyze my 41 beta sign ups and it helped me segmented them already. And we came up with a 4-wave plan to launch the beta to increase the likelihood of paid user conversions. Next step would be setting up the emails.

Though I was pulled away to chat with husband whole night, but I was able to figure out a solution for segmenting 40 beta testers.

lessons: ["Control my anger against a tantrum son. I should have done it better, yet I choose to tell at him again. I feel really bad. On top of that, maybe I should start the timer method. Teach me when to stop and play with my son,  and teach him to give me space to work"]
journal:
The bug ate everything. Again. Backbone bug don't wait for permission.
They show up in the morning and demand the whole day. No negotiation.

Raw Reflection:
Today was terrible.

Woke up to a crying child. Never figured out why. He just kept crying.

Woke up to bugs. Not just bugs—backbone bugs. Fixed all day. Still not fixed.

Then personal issues I had to deal with. Then nighttime, husband sent more bugs—his thoughts to improve the app.

I felt guilty all day. Neglecting my son while I sat here. But after thinking about it, his tantrum wasn't about me. He was just having a hard day too. Two humans having a hard day in the same house, both crying at different times.

The guilt still sits here though. Even knowing it wasn't my fault, it's still heavy.

Email automation? Didn't touch it. Beta launch? Probably needs to move. I'll figure that out tomorrow. Not tonight.

Right now I'm just one exhausted mom, trying to fix one more bug before I finally let myself sleep. Ended up sleeping at 2am

wins: ["Spent almost 5 good at the cafe. We ate, worked, chatted. ","We ate, worked, chatted. Progress was great, did more than I expected."]
lessons: ["Would write my evening reflection earlier and not before bed when my mind is all cramped. "]
journal:
Spent almost 5 good at the cafe. We ate, worked, chatted. Progress was great, did more than I expected.



But at night right before I sleep, my self doubt starts to creep in again. 40 beta sign ups, would they really convert? How many? Should I send invite to all 40 at once? Send all 40 would put all of them at risk of retention rate due to potential bugs, but batch them might lose time, patience. What if I batch them up, but first few rounds nobody signs up and I get discouraged too soon?

Transformation Video is good to go (finally) but I am sure it will need more adjusting later on. App debug finally not going in circles and we fixed each issue one by one.

wins: ["30 days of building app, community, and new relationship with myself","30 days challenge completed, 111 twitter followers, 38 beta sign ups, a fully working app"]
journal:
I listened to Mrs. Deer, I finish enough version of the app and close the laptop. Let the rest of the day be a rest day. I was watching tv with husband at 11pm, but I got really tired, first night of many nights I got tired and went to bed early instead. It's probably a closed chapter feeling. I did good, I like it, let the unknown future unfolds itself, but at least I won't regret it, as I have my best shot.
Though I see a tiny bug in the app, I'll fix that tomorrow.
I didn't do any Outreach during crack times also, let my brain rest up a little. 

30 days reflection: 
30 days ago, I started a public build thinking it was "worth a try."

I didn't know it would change everything.

---

Day 3: I saw a tiny transformation. It felt good.

Day 7: I couldn't stop talking to my husband about how this system was shifting something inside me.

Day 10: I fell in love. Not with building. With what I was building.

For the first time, I believe in what I'm making.

---

That's the difference between this business and my previous ones.

Before, I was building things I thought would work.
Things I hoped other people would want.
Things that made sense on paper but never lit me up inside.

This time? I'm building something I *need*.
Something that pulled me out of overwhelm.
Something that helped me see my own patterns for the first time.

And because I use it every day, I know exactly:
• What works
• What doesn't
• What my customers are actually looking for
• What pain points keep them stuck

I'm not guessing anymore. I'm building from experience.

---

The biggest shift wasn't visible from the outside.

Inside this system, "turn today's actions into better decisions" stopped being a slogan.

It became real.

My thoughts aren't random anymore. They're all bits and pieces of a bigger picture.

I know what I'm doing.
I know how each small step fits.
I know how to test one marketing channel at a time.
I know when something isn't working and it's time to pivot.
I know how to put myself back together when I have to.

All because of this system.

---

30 days.

111 Twitter followers.
38 beta sign-ups.
A working app I never thought I could build.
A system I will never stop using.
A husband who now believes in what I'm doing.

---

But the real accomplishment?

I walked into 30 days full of unknowns.
I tackled them one by one.
I balanced it with my family.
And I came out the other side proud of myself.

Small pebbles along the way.
But they all point in the same direction now.

Building Wheel of Founders to turn 30 days into a lifetime of clarity.
